results.possibleIdentity: Iris Crump (alternatively identified as Iris Krump) is an African American artist, sculptor, and printmaker who was a prominent figure in the New York art and activism scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. She is best known as a founding or early member of the collective "Where We At" Black Women Artists (WWA), established in 1971, and was active in the Art Workers Coalition (AWC). Her artistic style often involved the use of mixed media, specifically incorporating lights and mirrors into contemporary settings to depict communal human activities. Crump was deeply involved in social and institutional activism; in 1969, she participated in the AWC's "Open Hearing" and was part of a group that advocated for a Martin Luther King Jr. Wing for Black and Puerto Rican Art at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). She is also associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) milieu, appearing in archival and bibliographic records alongside technological and experimental art groups of that era. Her career extended into the mid-1980s, evidenced by her receipt of a National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) fellowship for Printmaking/Drawing/Artists' Books in 1985.
